natural_occurrence.description: Talc is a naturally occurring mineral, the softest mineral on the Mohs hardness scale. Food-grade talc must be purified and certified asbestos-free, as talc deposits can naturally occur near asbestos minerals.

manufacturing.description: Mined from talc deposits, then crushed, purified, and milled to fine powder. Food-grade and pharmaceutical-grade talc undergo rigorous testing to ensure absence of asbestos fibers. The purification process removes impurities and potential contaminants.

other_uses.cosmetics: Talcum powder (baby powder, body powder), makeup (absorbent, texture), dry shampoo.
other_uses.industrial: Paper coating, plastics, rubber, ceramics, paints.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Tablet and capsule lubricant, dusting powder.
other_uses.household: Baby powder (increasingly phased out due to contamination concerns), anti-chafing powder.

eu.conditions: Must be asbestos-free and meet purity standards. Permitted as release agent and anti-caking agent in specific foods.

usa.notes: Must be asbestos-free. Limited use in food applications.

japan.notes: Used in food processing with asbestos-free certification required.

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: not specified (acceptable for use when asbestos-free)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (safe at current use levels when asbestos-free)

regulatory_history.event: Increased scrutiny of talc products due to asbestos contamination concerns in cosmetic talcum powder. Food-grade talc requires strict asbestos-free certification. Several major brands discontinued talc-based baby powder.
Johnson & Johnson discontinued talc-based baby powder in North America due to declining sales and litigation over asbestos contamination concerns (cosmetic grade, not food grade).

fun_facts: Used in both BABY POWDER and CHEWING GUM - same mineral, different applications!
The softest mineral on Earth - rates 1 on the Mohs hardness scale (diamonds are 10)
Makes white rice shiny and attractive - the polished look comes from talc coating
CONTROVERSIAL: Cosmetic talc (baby powder) has faced lawsuits over asbestos contamination - food-grade must be certified asbestos-free
Talc naturally occurs near asbestos deposits, which is why rigorous testing is essential
Also known as 'French chalk' and used by tailors to mark fabric
Soapstone (used for countertops and sculptures) is massive talc
Declining use in food due to public perception issues, despite food-grade safety when properly purified
